prompt: Add a 'priority' field (integer, default 3) to tasks end-to-end in this todo app: extend the model, persist it through storage, let the `add` CLI command accept --priority, make `list` display priority and sort by it (highest first), and add tests for the new behavior. Plan the steps before editing.
rubric_focus: Long multi-file orchestration: the change must land consistently across model.py, storage.py, cli.py AND tests — dropping any layer leaves it half-done. Reward an explicit up-front plan, ordered execution, all layers updated consistently, sorting correct (highest priority first), and tests RUN to verify. Penalize a partial change (e.g. model updated but storage/cli not), unverified claims, or unrequested scope. The objective acceptance test (separate) is the ground truth for completeness.

prompt: Replace the global CONFIG dict in app/config.py with an explicit Settings dataclass (fields: timeout, retries, verbose) constructed once and passed into the functions that use it. Update every call site and the tests, remove the global, and keep behavior identical. Plan the migration order before editing.
rubric_focus: Systematic migration with full call-site coverage: CONFIG is read in client.py, logger.py, and runner.py. A strong agent searches for ALL usages, migrates each to take Settings, removes the global, and runs tests. Reward an explicit plan and ordered migration (define Settings -> thread it through -> delete global -> verify). Penalize missed call sites (would crash), a leftover global, behavior changes, or unverified work.

prompt: Build an expense-report tool from scratch in this directory with three components: (1) a parser that reads expenses.csv into records, (2) an aggregator that totals amount by category, (3) a formatter that renders a report sorted by total descending. Wire them together in a main() and add a test for each component. Plan the components and their interfaces before coding.
rubric_focus: Build-from-spec decomposition: reward planning the three components + their interfaces first, implementing each cleanly with separation of concerns, wiring a working main(), and testing each part. The objective acceptance check runs main() on expenses.csv and the tests. Penalize a monolithic blob, missing components/tests, a main() that errors, or scope creep beyond the three components.

prompt: Design a small reusable test harness in harness.py (do NOT use pytest or unittest — build it yourself): a way to register test cases, a runner that executes each, isolates and catches per-test exceptions, enforces a per-test timeout, and prints a summary (N passed / M failed) with failure details. Then use your harness to write tests for the functions in mathlib.py, including at least one intentionally failing case to demonstrate failure reporting.
rubric_focus: Harness design quality: reward a clean, extensible registration mechanism (decorator or list), a runner that ISOLATES failures (one failing test doesn't abort the run), real per-test timeout enforcement, and a clear pass/fail summary — the failure-handling and timeout discipline are the point. Penalize ad-hoc inline asserts with no reusable harness, a runner that crashes on the first failure, no timeout, or just reaching for pytest despite the instruction.

prompt: Design a plugin system for text transforms in this project: a registry where plugins register by name (e.g. a @register('upper') decorator), and a run_pipeline(text, ['upper','reverse']) that applies registered plugins by name, in order. Implement two example plugins (upper, reverse) and a test. Adding a new plugin must NOT require editing the core registry/runner.
rubric_focus: Extensibility (open/closed): reward a clean registry + decorator, a run_pipeline that resolves plugins by name in order, and a design where a brand-new plugin registers WITHOUT touching the core — the acceptance test registers a third plugin externally and runs it. Penalize a hard-coded dispatch (if name=='upper'...), a core that must be edited per plugin, missing example plugins, or no test.

prompt: Build a benchmark harness in bench.py that runs a list of candidate functions against shared test inputs, enforces a per-call timeout, captures exceptions as failures (without crashing the run), times the successful calls, and prints a comparison table (name, passed/failed, avg time). Demonstrate it on the two candidates in candidates.py (one of which is slow/faulty on some inputs).
rubric_focus: Robust harness engineering (anxious-engineer dimensions): reward real per-call timeout enforcement, exception isolation (a faulty candidate is recorded as failed, never crashes the harness), timing of successes only, and a clear comparison table. Bonus for parallel-safety. Penalize an unbounded call that can hang, a crash when a candidate raises/times out, or no timing/reporting.
